observations: Clear annual seasonal cycle: a broad valley around mid-year (July-Sept) and a rise toward each winter (Dec-Feb).
High-frequency wiggle riding on the seasonal curve, consistent with a short repeating (e.g. weekly) cycle plus noise.
A recurring sharp spike every winter near January-February that rises well above the surrounding level and then reverts within a short window.
The magnitude of these recurring winter spikes grows year over year: roughly ~170 (2019), ~178 (2020), ~184 (2021), ~207 (2022), ~222 (2023).
The overall baseline level drifts gradually upward across the full history; each year's valley and peak sit higher than the prior year's.
Near the forecast origin (mid-2023) the series is on the descending side just after the latest winter spike, declining from ~180 toward ~160 with the wiggle still present.
The last spike (early 2023) is the largest in the record and has just reverted before the forecast origin.
visual.pattern_summary: An upward-drifting series with strong annual seasonality and a recurring, growing winter spike event that reverts each year.
hypotheses: A forecast trained only on a recent window could miss the recurring winter spike if that window does not span a full annual cycle including the spike.
Even if the spike is captured, its growing magnitude year-over-year could cause under-prediction of the next event's height.
Treating the latest large spike as a new persistent level rather than a temporary recurring event could bias the forecast upward.
The end-of-history descent could be over-extrapolated downward, missing the seasonal trough-then-recovery shape.
The gradual upward drift may be under- or over-projected depending on how much recent slope dominates.
uncertainties: Exact dates, duration, and cause of the recurring spikes cannot be determined from the image.
Whether the upward drift continues, levels off, or reverses beyond the origin is unknown.
Whether the spike growth will keep accelerating or saturate cannot be judged from training history alone.
The exact frequency of the high-frequency wiggle (weekly vs other) is not verifiable from the plot.
Whether any apparent level changes are permanent shifts or part of the annual cycle is not fully resolvable.

proposal.visual_first_rationale:
Visually, the chart shows a sharp, narrow spike appearing every winter (January–February window) that rises well above the surrounding seasonal curve and then fully reverts within a short window (~3 weeks). This spike is distinctly recurring and calendar-anchored — it is not a permanent level shift nor a gradual ramp. The spike magnitude grows year-over-year (~170 in 2019 to ~222 in 2023), suggesting the holiday prior needs some flexibility. The overall series has a gradual upward drift riding under a clear annual seasonal cycle, with high-frequency noise on top.

Reconciling with numerics: `recurring_event_summary.is_calendar_recurring == true` with confidence 1.0, dominant month = February, appearing in exactly the same ~Feb 12–Mar 4 window every year across all 5 years. The `candidate_event_blocks` confirm 5 closed blocks, each ~21 days, each with mean_excess ~42 units — consistent with the visual spike height above the seasonal baseline. The `transient_event_score` (0.43) is moderate and `permanent_shift_magnitude` (43.6) reflects the spike height being picked up as a pseudo-shift, not a true permanent regime change. The naive full-history model already achieves low MAE (5.65) but cannot explicitly model the growing spike as a holiday effect — tuning holidays + priors can better encode this recurring calendar event and its scale.
proposal.expected_effect: By encoding the recurring Feb 12–Mar 4 winter spike as an explicit holiday regressor with a tuned holidays_prior_scale, the model will correctly attribute the annual spike to a calendar event rather than noise or trend. Using multiplicative seasonality mode will allow the spike magnitude to scale with the rising baseline (explaining the year-over-year growth). A moderate changepoint_prior_scale (0.05) and wider changepoint_range (0.9) will capture the gradual upward drift without over-fitting. This should reduce forecast error on the spike period and the post-spike descent, beating the naive full-history MAE of 5.65.

proposal.visual_first_rationale:
Visually, the chart shows a clear, recurring sharp spike every winter near January–February that rises well above the surrounding seasonal level and then reverts within a short window (~3 weeks). This pattern repeats identically in shape across all five years visible (2019–2023), with each spike occurring at the same calendar position (mid-February to early March). The spike magnitude grows year-over-year (~170 to ~222), but the reversion is clean each time — the series returns to the seasonal baseline. This is the defining visual signature of a recurring calendar holiday/event, not a permanent shift or a gradual drift.

Reconciling with numerics: `recurring_event_summary.is_calendar_recurring = true` with confidence 1.0, dominant_month = 2, and five perfectly spaced ~365-day windows (2019-02-12 through 2023-03-04). The `candidate_event_blocks` confirm five closed blocks of 21–22 days each, all with nearly identical mean_excess (~41–43 units above baseline). The `transient_event_score` of 0.43 is moderate, but the calendar regularity is definitive. The `permanent_shift_magnitude` is large (43.6) but this reflects the spike amplitude, not a true permanent level change — the series reverts after each event. The naive `full_history` baseline already performs well (MAE 5.65), so the intervention must correctly model the holiday structure to beat it. The previously rejected attempt used multiplicative seasonality with high priors; this attempt uses additive seasonality (the spike excess is roughly constant ~41 units, not proportional to baseline) and a tighter changepoint_prior_scale to avoid over-fitting the gradual drift changepoints, while keeping holidays_prior_scale high to capture the spike amplitude.
proposal.expected_effect: By encoding the recurring February–March winter spike as a holiday with a tuned prior, the model will explicitly anticipate and correctly size the annual event rather than treating it as noise or a trend change. Additive seasonality mode reflects the roughly constant ~41-unit spike excess across years. A tighter changepoint_prior_scale (0.01) prevents the model from over-attributing the spike onset/offset to trend shifts, and high holidays_prior_scale (10.0) gives the holiday regressor enough flexibility to capture the spike magnitude. This should reduce forecast error below the naive full_history MAE of 5.65.
